segments.s373-g65: سر (head) + پیر جادوش (the old magician) forms the direct object phrase; Persian often places the object before the verb in literary register. بنهاد is a literary/past verb (نهاد with the poetic/old prefix ب-) meaning ‘placed/put’ (third person past). پیش is a locative/adverbial meaning ‘before / in the presence of’ with its referent (him) omitted, as is common in narrative poetry.
segments.s373-g66: کشنده is an agent noun ‘the killer’ or ‘slayer’ and here functions as the subject. بکشت is the past action ‘killed’ rendered in the archaic/epic form with the prefixed ب-; together they give ‘the slayer killed’. اینت is a dialectal/poetic demonstrative (‘this’/‘thus’) pointing to the foregoing deed, and آیین و کیش ‘rite and creed’ is a nominal predicate describing that deed; the copula is omitted (typical in poetry) so the sense is ‘this was the rite/creed.’
segments.s373-g1: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (dative). داد is the 3rd person singular past 'gave'. پس شاه is a temporal/subject phrase 'then the king' placed after the verb (poetic inversion is common). بهزاد را is the direct object marked by را; the object (Behzad) is the recipient of the thing given, whose identity is completed in the next segment.
segments.s373-g2: سپه جوشن is a compound noun 'army cuirass/armor' (سپه modifying جوشن). و simply links the second item. خود here is reflexive 'for himself' (marks that the following object is taken by the subject). پولاد را is the second object (steel) with را marking it definite; the verb from the previous segment governs both objects.
segments.s373-g3: پس شاه is 'then the king' (temporal + subject). کشته is a noun here 'the slain' (people killed). میان را is a locative/phrase 'in the middle' with را marking the phrase as the object of ببست. ببست is 3rd person past; here it takes 'کشته میان را' as its object meaning 'he bound/fastened/closed (the slain in the middle)'.
segments.s373-g4: سیه رنگ is an adjectival phrase 'black/dark in color' modifying the following object. بهزاد را is the definite object 'Behzad' (marked by را). برنشست is past 3rd singular 'he seated/placed (upon)' — the verb applies to بهزاد را, so the king (subject) seated Behzad, who is described as dark-colored.
segments.s373-g5: خرامید is the past form 'he strode/pranced/strutted' describing movement. تا functions here as a limitative 'as far as/to' marking the destination. رزمگاه سپاه is a genitive construction 'the battlefield of the army' (رزمگاه governed by سپاه). The verb governs the extent phrase: 'he strode to/as far as the army's battlefield.'
segments.s373-g6: نشسته is a participial/adjectival form 'seated' describing someone present there. بران is an archaic adverb/pronoun 'upon that/thereon' indicating location. خوب رنگ سیاه is a descriptive noun phrase 'of fine/beautiful black color' (خوب qualifying رنگ, followed by the color سیاه). Together this describes someone seated there who is of handsome black hue.
segments.s373-g7: به پیش صف دشمنان is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of the line of the enemies' (به + پیش + صف + دشمنان). ایستاد is the 3rd person past 'he stood' (i.e., took his stand) and completes the clause: 'he stood before the enemy ranks.'
segments.s373-g8: همی is an archaic/perfective particle often marking continued or emphatic action (here giving a presentive/emphatic feel). برکشید is past 'he raised/drew up' (poetically 'he drew forth' — can apply to drawing a weapon or producing a sound/air). از introduces the source 'from'; جگر here is figurative 'heart/seat of feeling' (ablative 'from his heart'); سرد باد is the noun phrase 'cold wind' (سرد qualifying باد). The clause reads 'he drew forth, from his heart, a cold wind.'
segments.s373-g25: که is the subordinating/relative particle 'that' introducing this clause. نزد من آمد is literal word order (prepositional نزد + pronoun + past verb) meaning 'came to me / into my presence' (آمد is simple past). زریر is the proper name (subject) placed after the verb in the poetic word order. از نخست is an adverbial phrase 'from the first' = 'at first/initially', with از marking the source/onset and نخست giving the temporal sense.
segments.s373-g26: برین سان is the fused phrase بر + این + سان (بر این سان) meaning 'in this manner'; همی is the archaic verbal particle used in epic poetry to mark the ongoing/punctual action in narrative before the verb; تاخت is the past tense verb 'charged/galloped'. باره درست functions adverbially — باره 'instance/occasion' with درست 'exactly/properly' giving the sense 'straight/just so'.
segments.s373-g27: کجا رفت is the interrogative 'where did (he) go' (رفت = past of رفتن). آن بیدرفش گزین: آن 'that' + بیدرفش 'without a banner' (an epithet) + گزین used adjectivally 'chosen/select' (poetic epithet) — the noun/epithet sequence follows the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s373-g28: هم‌اکنون 'immediately/right now' sets the time. سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. منش here likely refers to a person/place called in the narrative (not one of the given noun senses), so no supplied sense fits (marked -1). خوانید is the 3rd-person plural past 'they called/cried' (here 'they cried out to X'), and هین is the interjection 'Hīn' (an archaic exclamation, 'behold/hey' or a call to stop/attention).
segments.s373-g29: بخواندند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they called' (the prefix ب here is the epic/historical present particle combined with past-form usage in poetry, but the whole form functions as 'they cried/summoned'). و is the coordinating 'and'. آمد is past 'came'. دمان appears as a proper name in this context (so it functions as the subject), and بیدرفش again is the epithet 'without banner' applied to that person.
segments.s373-g30: گرفته is the perfect/past participle 'having taken' or 'seized' describing the subject. به دست گرفته is the fixed phrase 'taken into the hand / held in the hand' (به + دست + گرفته). آن درفش بنفش is the object 'that purple standard' (آن = that, درفش = banner/standard, بنفش = purple). Word order places the participle before the prepositional phrase that specifies how/with what he came.
segments.s373-g31: نشسته is a past participle 'seated/sitting'. بران is a contracted prepositional phrase بر + آن ('upon that'). باره here is 'load/burden' (often used for saddle/pack in epic contexts), and خسروی is an adjective 'royal/regal' modifying باره, so the whole phrase means 'seated upon that royal burden/saddle'.
segments.s373-g32: بپوشیده is the past participle 'clothed/covered' (he was clad). آن جوشن پهلوی = 'that jošn (cuirass) Pahlavi' — جوشن 'cuirass/armor' and پهلوی here identifies the style/ethnic attribution (Pahlavi/Pahlavian); the phrase means 'that Pahlavi cuirass'. The order is participle + object (typical in poetry).
segments.s373-g57: نکو رنگ باره is an adjectival phrase describing good appearance or fine color (a poetic compound: نکو + رنگ + باره). Here it qualifies the following proper name and noun: زریر و درفش = “Zarrir and the standard.” Word order is modifier(s) before the nouns, typical in Persian poetry; باره functions as a formative element making رنگ into a descriptive compound.
segments.s373-g58: ببرد is a past-tense, 3rd-person form of بردن with the prefix ب- (classical narrative past: “he took/carried away”). The coordinating و links this clause to the next noun phrase. سر بی‌هنر is a noun (سر) modified by the adjective phrase بی‌هنر (“the unskilled one”); بیدرفش (بی‌درفش) is an adjectival/compound phrase meaning “without a banner,” describing that head/person.
segments.s373-g59: سپاه کیان is a genitival phrase: سپاه (host/army) governed by کیان (the Kian/kings). بانگ برداشتند uses برداشتند (here idiomatically) with بانگ as its object: برداشتند بانگ = “they raised/uttered a cry.” The verb is plural past (they).
segments.s373-g60: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here reinforcing the action: “indeed/altogether” or continuous sense). نعره از ابر بگذاشتند: نعره (shout) with the preposition از + ابر (“from the clouds”), and بگذاشتند (classical use of گذاشتن) here means “let out” or “sent forth” (they let a shout out). Word order places the adverb before the verbal clause.
segments.s373-g61: که introduces a result/narrative clause (“that/so/when”). پیروز شد شاه is a simple predication: پیروز (victorious) + شد (became) + شاه (the king). The coordinating و links the next clause in which دشمن فگند uses فگند (an older past-tense verb) meaning the enemy were driven off/scattered; the verb form is active/past and describes the enemy’s dispersal.
segments.s373-g62: بشد here is a narrative particle/auxiliary (classical use: “then/it so happened”), followed by بازآورد (past tense: he brought back/returned). The direct object sequence اسپ سمند is a doubled appositive (اسپ = steed, سمند = war-horse) specifying the animal that was brought back.
segments.s373-g63: شد at the start is past-tense auxiliary “became” (classical narrative). آن شاهزاده is the topical noun phrase “that prince,” and سوار دلیر should be read together: سوار as predicate complement meaning “mounted” (or ‘a rider’) and دلیر as the adjective “brave.” The construction means “that prince became mounted/brave rider.”
segments.s373-g64: سوی شاه gives direction/goal (“toward the king”), followed by the verb برد (here classical past ‘took/carried’) and the object آن سمند زریر = “that steed, Zarrir.” Word order is directional phrase → verb → object; the proper name زریر identifies the steed.
segments.s373-g17: وزان = و + زان, a connective prepositional phrase 'and from that' linking to the previous action; سوی دیگر is a noun phrase 'the other side/direction' with سوی (direction/side) followed by its modifier دیگر; گو is an introductory particle here meaning 'they say' / 'so-called' placed before a name as a narrative marker; اسفندیار is the proper name (the person introduced). The whole chunk positions Esfandiyar as coming from 'that other side'.
segments.s373-g18: همی is the classical imperfect/progressive marker ('was / kept on'), here giving an ongoing sense to the action; کشتشان is the verb کشت (kill) with the enclitic ـشان 'them' as its object (verb before clitic, typical in older poetic word order); بی‌مر is an archaic/intensifying adjective (uncertain/obsolete meaning here) paired with بی‌شمار 'countless' as adverbial modifiers describing the extent of killing. Word order: verb + object + adverbial qualifiers.
segments.s373-g19: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'; سالار چین is a noun phrase 'the commander (سالار) of China (چین)', with the modifier following the head; دید is the simple past 'saw / beheld'; بستور را is the definite object marked by را — here the name (or reference) Bostur is what was seen, so را closes the clause.
segments.s373-g20: کیان زاده is a compound meaning 'born of the Kayan/royal house' (a lineage adjective); آن پهلوان پور is an appositive phrase 'the son (پور) of that (آن) champion (پهلوان)' — پور is a postnominal element meaning 'son/descendant'. The whole phrase is in apposition to بستور (it describes him) and ends with را as an emphatic/definite marker.
segments.s373-g21: به لشکر بگفت = 'he said to the army': به marks the indirect object ('to/for'), لشکر is 'the army', and بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said / he told'. این‌که introduces the reported clause ('this that / that'), and شاید is a modal 'perhaps' introducing uncertainty. بدن begins the subordinate clause as the noun 'body' (object of the following verb phrase).
segments.s373-g22: کزین سان = کَ + زین + سان, 'from/by this manner' or 'in this way'; همی again gives a progressive/continuative sense; نیزه داند زدن is a periphrastic construction where داند (from دانستن) 'know' takes the infinitive زدن 'to strike' — 'know how to strike with a spear.' In other words, the clause means 'they may know how to thrust spears in this manner.'
segments.s373-g23: بکشت is an archaic past form (verb preceding subject, common in epic diction) meaning 'slew/killed'; از تگینان is a partitive 'of/from the Tegins' (تگینان plural of the title Tegin); من is the subject pronoun 'I' placed after the verb (older word order); بی‌شمار is an adjective/adverb 'countless' describing the extent of the killing.
segments.s373-g24: مگر here means 'except' (introducing the exception to the preceding statement); گشت is the past 'became / was' (here 'remained/turned out to be'); زنده is the predicate adjective 'alive'; زریر سوار is the name plus title 'Zarihr the rider/horseman' that forms the exception: 'except that Zarihr the horseman remained alive.' The verb agrees with the following predicate phrase.
segments.s373-g33: خرامید is a past tense 3rd‑person singular verb meaning 'he strode/advanced proudly'. تا پیش لشکر is a short prepositional phrase: تا here = 'up to/until' and پیش لشکر = 'before/in front of the army' (پیش + direct noun). ز شاه uses the poetic preposition ز (from/of) to mark possession: 'of the king' (i.e. 'the king's ...'). Word order is verb → extent/place → possessor.
segments.s373-g34: نگهبان مرز و نگهبان گاه is a nominal apposition describing a role: نگهبان = 'guard/guardian', مرز = 'border/frontier', and گاه ('gāh') here names the post/station (classically also 'time') — thus 'guardian of the border and guardian of the post'. The two نگهبان phrases are linked by و 'and'.
segments.s373-g35: گرفته is a past participial/past form meaning 'having taken/holding' (here implying 'he had taken'). همان 'the same/that very' emphasizes the specific sword (تیغ = 'sword'). زهر آبدار is a compound adjective: زهر 'poison' + آبدار literally 'juicy/moist' used figuratively 'poison‑steeped' or 'poison‑laden'.
segments.s373-g36: که begins a relative clause 'which/who'. افگنده is a perfective/past participle of افگَندن 'to throw/strike/down' here meaning 'had cast down/struck (down)'. بد is an attributive adjective 'bad/evil' qualifying the following name; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; زریر سوار = 'Zarihr the rider' (name + occupational/epithet). The clause therefore qualifies the sword as the one that had felled that evil rider.
segments.s373-g37: بگشتند is past plural 'they turned/returned'. هر دو 'both' modifies the subject. به before the nouns introduces the items they took up/returned to ('to' or 'toward' their equipment): ژوپین = gambeson (armour jacket) and تیر = 'arrow' (here 'bows/arrows' as weapons). The verb governs these objects.
segments.s373-g38: سر here means 'chief/head' (sense 'leader') and is followed by جادوان, the plural/collective 'magicians' used adjectivally ('of the magicians' or 'magician-chief'). ترک و پور زریر are two names/appositions: ترک (here a person/name) and پور زریر = 'the son/descendant of Zarihr' (پور as patronymic). Together they rename or identify the two who 'turned back'.
segments.s373-g39: پس 'then' gives temporal sequencing. آگاه کردند is the verbal phrase 'they made (someone) aware' = 'they informed' (آگاه here forms the object/complement of کردن). زان کارزار = 'of that battle' (زان = 'of/from that', کارزار = 'battle') — this is the information content being reported.
segments.s373-g40: پس again marks sequence/emphasis. شاه را is the definite object/topic marker: شاه 'king' with را showing the person who is being informed (the indirect/affected party). فرخ اسفندیار is an appositive identifying the king by name (فرخ = epithet 'fortunate', اسفندیار = proper name).
segments.s373-g49: زدش is the past verb زدن with the enclitic object -ش (’him’): “he struck him.” پهلوانی یکی is a compact, poetic phrase meaning ‘a (single) heroic [blow/hero]’ (word order is inverted for meter), followed by the prepositional phrase بر جگر marking the target (‘on/to the liver/heart’). The whole segment reads as a single action: ‘a champion/heroic blow struck him once on the jigar.’
segments.s373-g50: چنان کز introduces a result clause: چنان = ‘so/thus’ and کز (classical contraction of که + ز) = ‘that/so that.’ دگر سو = ‘the other side’ (دگر = other, سو = side). برون کرد is verb + adverb: برون = ‘out’ and کرد (here causative/simple past ‘made/caused’) takes سر (‘head’) as its object; the clause means ‘so that he caused the head to go out the other side.’
segments.s373-g51: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction ‘when.’ آهو is the subject ‘the gazelle.’ ز باره is a prepositional/causal phrase ‘from/through the bāre’ (here ‘the charge/assault’), and در افتاد is the compound verb ‘fell down/into’ (افتاد past). The coordinating و joins مرد, where مرد is used as the past tense ‘died’ (verb), even though the provided senses list gives only noun senses.
segments.s373-g52: بدید is the past tense ‘he saw.’ از before کیان زادگان marks the agent/source and here functions like ‘by/among’ or ‘from’ (classical usage). کیان زادگان = ‘the offspring/house of the Kians’ (Kayanian royal stock), and دستبرد is the noun ‘seizure/encroachment/raid’ — so the phrase reads ‘he saw a seizure/raid by the Kayan-born.’
segments.s373-g53: فرود آمد is a verbal phrase ‘came down / alighted / fell’ (فرود = descent/alighting, آمد = came). از باره اسفندیار is a causal/ablative phrase ‘from/by the attack of Esfandiyar’ explaining the cause of the falling.
segments.s373-g54: This is a compact noun phrase: سلیح زریر = ‘Zarrir’s weapon’ (poetic word order with the possessive relation shown without an explicit ezāfe), and آن گزیده سوار is an appositive/epithet ‘that chosen rider/horseman.’ In other words, ‘Zarrir’s weapon fell — that chosen horseman’s (weapon).’
segments.s373-g55: ازان = ‘from that’ (referring back), جادوی پیر = ‘the magic/sorcery of the old man’ (جادوی = magic, پیر = old man/elder), and بیرون کشید is the compound verb ‘drew out / pulled out’ (بیرون = out, کشید = past of کشیدن ‘to pull’). The verb anticipates its object (سرش را) in the next segment.
segments.s373-g56: سرش را is the direct object ‘his head’ (سر + possessive clitic -ش, and را the definite object marker). ز نیمه‌تن اندر برید is a prepositional + verbal phrase: ز = ‘from,’ نیمه‌تن = ‘half-body’ (a body only partly formed or half the body), اندر = ‘in/within,’ and برید is the past of بریدن ‘to cut’ meaning ‘cut off/cleaved.’ The whole clause describes cutting the head from the half-bodied torso.
segments.s373-g9: This is a speaker-introduction line: منم (literally “I too / I myself”) is a first-person emphatic phrase; گفت here is the narrative past “said” (the lemma senses list omitted the verbal sense), and بستور پور زریر is a name plus patronymic (پور = “son [of]”) placed after the personal name — a common poetic ordering. The whole segment reports the speech: “I too, Bastur son of Zarir, said…”.
segments.s373-g10: پذیره is a noun meaning ‘reception/ shelter’; نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive/optative form meaning ‘does not / will not come’; the clitic object/beneficiary مرا is broken here orthographically into م + را (an older/poetic splitting), so م…را functions as ‘to me’/‘for me’ and is marked by the object particle را. نره شیر is a noun phrase ‘male lion’ with the modifier نره placed before شیر (adjective/epithet before noun, typical in poetry). Together: ‘no reception/shelter comes to me — (I am) a male lion,’ i.e. ‘I will not accept shelter; I am a male lion.’
segments.s373-g11: کجا is the interrogative ‘where’. باشد is a present/subjunctive form of ‘to be’ used here to mean ‘is/exists’ (a polite/poetic present). آن is the demonstrative ‘that’, and جادوی is the noun ‘magic’ or ‘sorcery’ (the -ی ending marks the noun/adjectival relationship). بیدرفش is a compound epithet: بی- ‘without’ + درفش ‘banner/standard’, i.e. ‘the bannerless’ or ‘without-banner’ used as a modifier of جادو (a set epithet in the poem).
segments.s373-g12: که is the relative pronoun ‘that/which’ linking to the preceding noun (جادوی بیدرفش). بردست here represents the prepositional phrase بر دست ‘in/on the hand of’ (i.e. ‘held by’). آن جمشیدی درفش is ‘that Jamshidi banner/standard’ — جمشیدی is an adjectival form relating to Jamshid, and درفش is ‘banner’. The whole relative clause specifies where/with whom that magic once was: ‘which was in the hand of that Jamshidi standard.’
segments.s373-g13: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. 'پاسخ ندادند' is the past negative (3rd person plural) of دادن — literally 'they did not give (an) answer'. The object 'آزاد را' is marked by the postpositive object marker 'را'; in this poetic word order the verb precedes the object (verb … object) instead of the more colloquial 'به آزاد پاسخ ندادند.'
segments.s373-g14: 'برانگیخت' is the simple past (3rd singular) meaning 'roused/awoke/raised'. In this line the subject 'شبرنگ' follows the verb (a common poetic inversion), and 'بهزاد را' is the definite direct object marked by 'را' — 'he roused Behzād.'
segments.s373-g15: 'بکشت' is an archaic/political past form meaning 'he slew' (literary prefix ب- + کشت(ن)); the following prepositional phrase 'از تگینان' means 'from among the Teginān' (Tegin = a title/elite), and 'لشکر بسی' is a noun ('army/host') followed by the quantifier 'بسی' (a poetic postpositive meaning 'much/many'), so together 'he slew many of the Teginān's host.'
segments.s373-g16: 'پذیره نیامد' is a negated past construction meaning 'no reception/refuge came' or 'was not given'. The short pronoun 'مر' is an archaic dative-like particle (here functioning as 'for/unto him'), 'او را' is 'him' marked by 'را', and 'کسی' at the end (in this negative context) means 'no one' — together: 'no one came to give him shelter.'
segments.s373-g41: همی is a continual/aspectual particle meaning "kept on"; تاختش is the past tense verb تاخت (to gallop/charge) with the enclitic -ش ("him") attached, so the clause reads literally "he kept charging him." تا introduces a temporal limit meaning "until," and بدیشان (بـ + ایشان) is a prepositional pronominal phrase "to/at them," followed by رسید (past "reached"). The clause thus means "he kept charging him until he reached them."
segments.s373-g42: سر جادوان is a nominal phrase with سر in the figurative sense "chief/head" (i.e. "chief of the enchanters"). چون here functions as the temporal conjunction "when/as." مر is an archaic dative/accusative form meaning "me/to me" (an indirect object), او را is the explicit direct object with را marking definiteness, and بدید is the simple past "saw." Word order is flexible: the time-clause introduced by چون comes before the verb, and the enclitic/object pronoun (مر) appears before او را.
segments.s373-g43: برافگند is a past-tense verb meaning "threw off/away" (verb precedes its object as common in classical Persian). اسپ is the direct object "steed/horse." از میان نبرد is a prepositional/adverbial phrase "out of/from the midst of the battle" (از میان = "from among," نبرد = "battle").
segments.s373-g44: بدانست is the past tense "he knew/realized." What follows is a nominal/clausal phrase describing what he realized: کش here is a terse, poetic noun (used agentively/intensifyingly) often meaning a violent pull/force or stroke; بر سر is the prepositional phrase "upon the head," افتاد is the past "fell," and مرد is the agent/subject "man." Classical Persian often places the subject after the verb, so the literal order yields "he realized: a (force/a) man fell upon the head."
segments.s373-g45: بینداخت is the simple past "he cast/threw." آن زهر خورده is a noun phrase: آن "that," زهر "poison," خورده the past participle "eaten/swallowed" (so "that which had swallowed/contained poison"). به روی is a directional phrase "onto the face" (به = to/onto, روی = face). The phrase denotes throwing the poisonous matter toward someone's face.
segments.s373-g46: مگر introduces a rhetorical/exclusive question here ("who, then...?/could anyone..."). کس is the indefinite "anyone/any person." کند is the present/subjunctive form of کردن used in a rhetorical question meaning "would do/could do." زشت is the adjective "ugly" used as object complement, رخشنده "radiant/bright-faced" modifies روی "face." The construction is essentially "Could anyone make the radiant face ugly?" — note the adjective (زشت) as result/complement of zrobić (کند).
segments.s373-g47: نیامد here carries the idiomatic sense "it was not fitting/it did not succeed" (a classical Persian usage rather than literal "did not come"). برو is a short element here linked to the following noun phrase (in poetic syntax it can mark direction or emphasis), تیغ is "sword/blade," and زهر آبدار is a compound modifier: زهر "poison" + آبدار (literally "juicy/moist," used figuratively to intensify the poisonous quality). The phrase thus says that the poisonous blade did not have the intended effect/was not appropriate.
segments.s373-g48: گرفتش is the past tense "he seized/ took it," with the object clitic -ش attached. همان is a demonstrative "that very/the same," تیغ "sword," شاه "of the king" (genitive attribute), and استوار is an adjective "steadfast/firm" modifying شاه (here forming the epithet "the steadfast king" or "the king's steadfast sword"). Word order places the verb before the noun phrase in classical style: "he seized it — that same sword of the steadfast king."

sentences.s373-s33: He placed the old sorcerer's head before him; the slayer killed — such was the rite and the creed.
sentences.s373-s1: Then the king gave Behzad the army cuirass and kept the steel for himself.
sentences.s373-s2: Then the king bound the slain in the midst and set Behzad, dark in color, upon [it].
sentences.s373-s3: He strode to the army's battlefield; there sat upon it one of handsome black color.
sentences.s373-s4: He stood before the enemy ranks and then drew forth, from his heart, a cold wind.
sentences.s373-s13: Zarir came up to me at first; then, in that very manner, he charged straight on.
sentences.s373-s14: Where did that bannerless, chosen one go? Immediately they cried toward Mansh: 'Hīn!'
sentences.s373-s15: They cried out, and Daman, the bannerless one, came — having taken that purple standard in his hand.
sentences.s373-s16: Seated on that royal saddle/burden, he was clad in that Pahlavi cuirass.
sentences.s373-s29: He carried off Zarrir’s fine-hued garment and the standard, and took the head of the unskilled man, without a banner.
sentences.s373-s30: The Kian host raised a cry; indeed they let out a shout from the clouds.
sentences.s373-s31: The king became victorious and the enemy was scattered; then he brought back the steed, the war-horse.
sentences.s373-s32: That brave prince mounted, and brought that steed Zarrir toward the king.
sentences.s373-s9: And from that other side Esfandiyar, they say, was slaughtering them in untold numbers.
sentences.s373-s10: When the Chinese commander saw Bostur, the Kayan-born son of that champion.
sentences.s373-s11: He told the army this: 'Perhaps, for that reason, they know how to thrust spears in this way.'
sentences.s373-s12: I slew countless Tegins, but Zarihr the horseman survived.
sentences.s373-s17: He strode up to the front of the army, the king's guardian of the frontier and guardian of the post.
sentences.s373-s18: He had taken that very poison‑laden sword, which had struck down that evil rider Zarihr.
sentences.s373-s19: Both turned back to their gambesons and arrows — the chief of the magicians, Turk, and the son of Zarihr.
sentences.s373-s20: Then they informed the king, Farrokh Esfandiyar, of that battle.
sentences.s373-s25: A champion struck him once in the liver (heart), so that the head came out the other side.
sentences.s373-s26: When the gazelle, struck by the onslaught, fell into the fray and died.
sentences.s373-s27: From Esfandiyar’s assault, Zarrir’s weapon fell — that chosen horseman’s sword.
sentences.s373-s28: He drew him out of that old man’s sorcery, pulled out his head, and cut it off from the half of his body.
sentences.s373-s5: “I too,” Bastur, son of Zarir, said: “No shelter will come to me — (I am) a male lion.”
sentences.s373-s6: Where is that wonder-working magic of the bannerless [one] that was in the hand of that Jamshidi standard?
sentences.s373-s7: When they gave no answer to Āzād, Shabrang roused Behzād.
sentences.s373-s8: He slew many from the Teginān's host; no one came to give him refuge.
sentences.s373-s21: He kept charging until he reached them; when the chief of the enchanters saw him...
sentences.s373-s22: He flung the steed out from the midst of the fight; he perceived that a man had fallen upon (someone's) head.
sentences.s373-s23: He hurled that which had swallowed poison into his face; who, then, could make a radiant face ugly?
sentences.s373-s24: It was not fitting for that poisoned, sapid blade; he seized it — that very sword of the steadfast king.
